questions.prompt: "What are your plans for the holidays?" Which reply fits best?

options.text: I'm going to stay with my grandparents.
I will to stay with my grandparents.

questions.explanation: A plan you already made is expressed with be going to: I'm going to stay with my grandparents. "I'll stay" sounds like a decision made at this second, the present "I stay" isn't about the future, and "will to stay" adds a wrong to.

questions.prompt: "The soup looks delicious but there are no spoons." "Oh, ___ get some." (deciding now)
questions.explanation: Reacting on the spot to a problem is a will moment: I'll get some. "I'm going to" would suggest a plan you had earlier, "I going to" is missing the be (am), and "I will to" wrongly puts to after will.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Be careful! You ___ drop those plates!" (you can see it about to happen)
questions.explanation: A prediction from what you can see right now uses be going to: You are going to drop those plates! "will going to" stacks two futures, "are go to" breaks the form, and "will to" is never correct.

questions.prompt: Complete the question: "___ you going to invite Sara to the party?"
questions.explanation: A going-to question with you inverts be: Are you going to invite…? "Is" is for he/she/it, "Do" is the present-simple helper (not used with going to), and "Will" starts a different structure (Will you invite…?).

questions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence for a promise.

options.text: I'll pay you back on Friday, I promise.
I'll to pay you back on Friday, I promise.
I pay you back on Friday, I promise.
I will paying you back on Friday, I promise.

questions.explanation: A promise uses will + base verb: I'll pay you back. "I'll to pay" adds a wrong to, "I pay" is the present (not a future promise), and "will paying" uses the -ing form instead of the base verb.

questions.prompt: Complete the negative plan: "He ___ come to the meeting; he told us yesterday."
questions.explanation: A plan already known ("he told us yesterday") is negated with be going to + not, and with he that is isn't going to: He isn't going to come. "aren't going to" is plural, "don't going to" uses the wrong helper, and "won't to" adds a wrong to after won't.
